planting.note: Plant divisions or nursery-grown plants in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Labrador violet is an outstanding self-seeding groundcover for shaded areas, particularly valued for its purple-flushed foliage that adds color contrast to green shade plantings.

directions: Plant in spring or fall in moist, humus-enriched soil in partial to full shade; Labrador violet self-seeds freely once established and naturalizes into a charming, informal ground cover under trees and in shaded woodland gardens. Viola labradorica is a native North American violet of cool woodland, alpine, and subarctic habitats, distinguished from other violets by its distinctively purple-flushed to dark violet foliage — particularly striking in light shade where the leaves often take on a nearly burgundy coloration that contrasts beautifully with the tiny, lavender-purple flowers produced from early spring through summer. It is much smaller than most violet species, forming low mats only 3–6 inches tall that spread by self-seeding and gradually colonize an area; the heart-shaped leaves with their characteristic purple flush make it instantly recognizable among other shade groundcovers. An excellent, low-maintenance companion to hostas, ferns, and other shade perennials in zones 3–8.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; European columbine is a classic cottage garden perennial that thrives in partial shade and self-seeds freely, creating naturalized drifts in shaded borders and woodland edges over time.

directions: Sow seeds in fall directly where plants are to grow, pressing lightly onto the soil surface and allowing winter cold to stratify the seeds for spring germination; European columbine self-seeds readily and does not need to be replanted each year once established. Aquilegia vulgaris is the classic European columbine of cottage gardens and woodland edges, a well-loved perennial with delicate blue-green, clover-like compound leaves and nodding, spurless or short-spurred flowers in shades of blue-violet, purple, white, and pink from late spring through early summer; unlike many North American columbines with long spurs, the European species tends toward shorter spurs and double flowers in garden varieties. The 'Nora Barlow' cultivar with doubled, pompom flowers in red, pink, and white is particularly well-known. Columbines self-seed generously and will hybridize freely among themselves; seedlings may vary in color. Short-lived perennials (2–4 years) that renew themselves by seeding.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist to dry, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Geranium nodosum is exceptional among shade-tolerant hardy geraniums for its ability to bloom under dense tree canopy, in dry shade under shallow-rooted trees, and in difficult spots where other geraniums fail.

directions: Plant divisions in spring or fall in well-drained, moderately fertile to poor soil in partial to full shade; Geranium nodosum is remarkably adaptable to dry shade under mature trees — one of the few ornamental geraniums that thrives where other shade perennials struggle with root competition and summer drought. Geranium nodosum (knotted cranesbill) is a compact, rhizomatous hardy geranium native to mountain woodlands of southern Europe, producing deeply lobed, glossy, medium-green leaves and an extended succession of small but attractive funnel-shaped, lilac-pink to magenta flowers with white veining from late spring through midsummer and sometimes continuing into fall; it is evergreen or semi-evergreen in mild climates and one of the most shade-tolerant and drought-tolerant of all hardy geraniums once established. Particularly valued for its ability to thrive in 'problem shade' situations — dry, rooty, competition-laden zones under shallow-rooted trees like maples where most shade plants fail. Spreads slowly by rhizome without becoming invasive.
sources: Cranesbills Across The World — Peter Yeo

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; silvery glade fern is named for the silvery-white sori that cover the underside of fertile fronds in early summer, giving the fronds a distinctive shimmering appearance that makes it easy to identify.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in consistently moist, humus-enriched, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; silvery glade fern spreads slowly by rhizomes to form attractive colonies in moist woodland settings. Deparia acrostichoides (silvery glade fern, formerly Athyrium acrostichoides) is a native deciduous fern of eastern North American moist woodlands, producing broad, lance-shaped, softly textured fronds 18–30 inches long on pale, delicate stems; in early summer the undersides of the fronds are covered with elongated silver-white sori arranged in a distinctive herringbone pattern that gives the fern its common name and makes it one of the most beautiful of eastern woodland ferns when backlit. It thrives in the moist, humus-rich soil of stream banks, woodland margins, and shaded ravines, naturalizing readily by rhizome spreading into loose colonies. An underused native fern with elegant foliage and unique ornamental sori; attractive companion to trilliums, Solomon's seal, and hostas.

planting.note: Plant small divisions or pot-grown plants in spring in moist to moderately dry, rocky to humus-rich, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in partial to full shade; fragile fern is unusual in tolerating alkaline, rocky, and cliff-face conditions that most ferns cannot — it is often found growing in rock crevices and on mossy outcrops.

directions: Plant in spring in well-drained, moderately moist, rocky or humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; fragile fern is well suited to planting in rock garden crevices, along stone walls, on mossy banks, and in shaded rock gardens where its diminutive size suits the scale. Cystopteris fragilis is one of the most widely distributed ferns in the world, found across North America, Europe, Asia, and even South America wherever shaded, rocky slopes and cliff faces provide moisture-retaining crevices; the common name refers to the delicate, brittle stipes (stems) that snap easily when handled, not to the plant's constitution — it is a tough, cold-hardy fern tolerating zones 2–8. The fronds are small (6–15 inches), finely twice-pinnate, lacy, and light green, emerging in early spring and sometimes going dormant in summer drought; in a sheltered, consistently moist spot it may remain lush through the season. An excellent miniature fern for shaded rock gardens, wall crevices, and small-scale woodland plantings.
